claim: The collapse coding for the Western Roman Empire is contested by the transformation thesis: rather than collapsing, the Roman sacred political order was absorbed and transformed by successor Germanic kingdoms and the Catholic Church, which preserved and transmitted Roman institutional forms — suggesting absorption-transformation may be as accurate as collapse.
rationale: The Gibbon decline-and-fall narrative has been challenged by historians who argue the period 376-568 CE involved continuity and transformation rather than collapse; the choice between collapse and absorption-transformation codings has significant implications for what the case tests in the theory.
